 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-0bo","title":"fcp.docusign: Track Signature Status","description":"# fcp.docusign: Track Signature Status (FCP2)

## Goal
Allow agents to track envelope progress:
- delivered/signed/completed/declined/voided
- recipient-level status (without leaking PII)

## Scope
- Get envelope status.
- List envelopes with filters (date range, status).
- Optional: ingest Connect webhooks for real-time status updates.

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `docusign.envelopes.read`
- `docusign.webhook.ingest` (optional)

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned.
- PII hygiene:
  - recipient names/emails are sensitive
  - logs include only envelope_id + status + timestamps

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `docusign.envelope.status.get`
  - `docusign.envelope.list`
  - Optional: `docusign.webhook.ingest`

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - status parsing
  - webhook replay/idempotency (if enabled)

## Acceptance criteria
- Envelope status tracking is reliable and safe for automation.

Findings:
- `crates/fcp-e2e/src/subprocess.rs` already sets `Command::kill_on_drop(true)` in `ConnectorProcessRunner::spawn()`.
- The old manual-Drop approach is no longer present.
- A Unix regression test exists at `drop_reaps_long_running_process`, which checks that dropping a long-running runner makes its PID disappear.

Verification:
- `rch exec -- cargo check -p fcp-e2e --all-targets` passed.
- `rch exec -- cargo fmt -p fcp-e2e -- --check` passed.
- `ubs crates/fcp-e2e/src/subprocess.rs` is not trustworthy for this single Rust file in this repo state; UBS misclassified the path across multiple language scanners and emitted obvious false positives like JavaScript loose-equality warnings on Rust syntax.
- A targeted `rch exec -- cargo test -p fcp-e2e --lib drop_reaps_long_running_process -- --nocapture` did not reach the `fcp-e2e` test body because the remote build hit an unrelated compile blocker in `crates/fcp-conformance/src/vectors/hpke.rs` on the worker, despite the local worktree already containing a fix for that line.
- Earlier remote test runs also hit separate `rch` artifact-retrieval failures caused by pathological nested paths under `.beads/recovery_*`; that is operational fallout, not evidence against this bead implementation.

Conclusion: the requested `kill_on_drop` change and regression coverage are already present in the current worktree, so this bead is stale-complete from a backlog perspective. Remaining noise is in unrelated verification infrastructure.","created_at":"2026-03-25T19:32:26Z"}]}

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-0s6","title":"fcp.docusign: Add Recipients and Fields","description":"# fcp.docusign: Add Recipients and Fields (FCP2)

## Goal
Configure envelope recipients and signature fields (tabs) in a safe, typed way.

## Scope
- Add recipients (signer/CC/etc) with routing order.
- Add tabs/fields:
  - signature
  - initials
  - date signed
  - text fields
  - checkbox
- Support both:
  - coordinate-based placement
  - anchor-tag placement (preferred when possible)

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `docusign.envelopes.write`
- `docusign.envelopes.tabs.write`

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned.
- PII hygiene:
  - recipient emails/names are sensitive; never log
- Deterministic field placement:
  - stable IDs for recipient roles/tabs

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `docusign.envelope.add_recipient`
  - `docusign.envelope.update_recipient`
  - `docusign.envelope.add_tabs`

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - recipient routing rules
  - anchor-tag placement
  - redaction

## Acceptance criteria
- Envelopes can be configured reproducibly without leaking recipient PII.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-r1sl","title":"TEST: Connector SDK Unit Tests (Standard Methods, Typed Schemas, State Model)","description":"# TEST: Connector SDK Unit Tests

## Goal
Validate the connector SDK standard-method surface is correct and ergonomic via a comprehensive test suite (schemas, state, streaming).

## Scope
Comprehensive tests for connector SDK APIs and patterns.

## Test Categories

### Standard Methods
- [ ] invoke() method signature correct
- [ ] simulate() method signature correct (preflight; no side effects)
- [ ] subscribe() method signature correct
- [ ] get_state() method signature correct
- [ ] set_state() method signature correct
- [ ] introspect() returns correct schema

### Simulate / CostEstimate / Availability (Spec-aligned)

#### Core Simulate Tests
- [ ] SimulateRequest/SimulateResponse canonical encoding round-trip
- [ ] `estimate_cost=true` populates `CostEstimate` deterministically when supported
- [ ] `check_availability=true` populates `ResourceAvailability` deterministically when supported
- [ ] missing capability reporting is stable (ordering + content)
- [ ] simulate MUST NOT call write paths in the test connector (mock-enforced)
- [ ] simulate timeouts are enforced and do not leak secrets in error strings/logs

#### Simulate Golden Vectors
```rust
// tests/vectors/sdk/simulate_preflight_allow.cbor
SimulateRequest {
    id: ObjectId::from_hex("sim-001"),
    operation: "email.send",
    input: json\!({"to": "test@example.com", "subject": "Test"}),
    capability_token: Some(valid_token),
    estimate_cost: true,
    check_availability: true,
}
// Expected: would_succeed=true, estimated_cost populated

// tests/vectors/sdk/simulate_preflight_deny_capability.cbor
SimulateRequest {
    id: ObjectId::from_hex("sim-002"),
    operation: "email.send",
    input: json\!({"to": "test@example.com"}),
    capability_token: None,  // Missing
    estimate_cost: false,
    check_availability: false,
}
// Expected: would_succeed=false, missing_capabilities=["email.send"]

// tests/vectors/sdk/simulate_preflight_deny_policy.cbor
SimulateRequest {
    id: ObjectId::from_hex("sim-003"),
    operation: "storage.write",
    input: json\!({"path": "/sensitive/secrets.txt"}),
    capability_token: Some(valid_token),
    estimate_cost: false,
    check_availability: false,
}
// Expected: would_succeed=false, failure_reason="POLICY_DENIED: path_restricted"
```

#### CostEstimate Scenarios
```rust
#[test]
fn test_cost_estimate_deterministic() {
    // Given the same inputs, cost estimate must be identical
    let req = SimulateRequest { estimate_cost: true, ... };
    let r1 = connector.simulate(req.clone()).await?;
    let r2 = connector.simulate(req).await?;
    assert_eq\!(r1.estimated_cost, r2.estimated_cost);
}

#[test]
fn test_cost_estimate_no_pricing_tables() {
    // CostEstimate must NOT contain CurrencyCost unless connector
    // has explicit, stable currency basis
    let resp = connector.simulate(req).await?;
    if let Some(cost) = resp.estimated_cost {
        // api_credits, estimated_duration_ms, estimated_bytes are OK
        // currency must be None or explicitly stable
        assert\!(cost.currency.is_none() || cost.currency_is_stable);
    }
}

#[test]
fn test_availability_check_rate_limit_headroom() {
    // ResourceAvailability must include rate limit signals when available
    let req = SimulateRequest { check_availability: true, ... };
    let resp = connector.simulate(req).await?;
    if let Some(avail) = resp.availability {
        // Should report remaining/reset if the connector tracks rate limits
        assert\!(avail.rate_limit_remaining.is_some() || avail.no_rate_limit);
    }
}
```

#### Mock Enforcement Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_simulate_never_calls_write() {
    // Mock connector that panics if write paths are called
    let mock = MockConnector::new()
        .on_write(|| panic\!("simulate called write path\!"));
    let req = SimulateRequest { operation: "db.insert", ... };
    // This must NOT panic
    let resp = mock.simulate(req).await?;
    assert\!(\!resp.would_succeed || resp.is_dry_run);
}

#[test]
fn test_simulate_timeout_no_secret_leak() {
    // Ensure timeout errors do not include request data
    let req = SimulateRequest {
        input: json\!({"secret_api_key": "sk-12345"}),
        ...
    };
    let err = connector.simulate_with_timeout(req, Duration::from_millis(1))
        .await
        .unwrap_err();
    assert\!(\!err.to_string().contains("sk-12345"));
    assert\!(\!format\!("{:?}", err).contains("sk-12345"));
}
```

### Typed Schemas
- [ ] Operation schema generation
- [ ] Event schema generation
- [ ] State schema generation
- [ ] Schema validation at runtime
- [ ] Schema evolution rules (V2-only; fail-closed + explicit migration)
  - unknown schema hashes rejected deterministically
  - interface_hash changes are detectable and surfaced via introspection
  - migrations (if any) are explicit and produce new content-addressed objects

#### Schema Golden Vectors
```rust
// tests/vectors/sdk/operation_schema_v1.json
{
  "schema_id": "urn:fcp:schema:email.send:v1",
  "schema_hash": "blake3:abc123...",
  "input": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
      "to": {"type": "string", "format": "email"},
      "subject": {"type": "string", "maxLength": 998},
      "body": {"type": "string"}
    },
    "required": ["to", "subject"]
  },
  "output": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
      "message_id": {"type": "string"}
    }
  }
}

// tests/vectors/sdk/schema_evolution_reject.json
// When interface_hash changes, must be explicit
{
  "old_hash": "blake3:abc123...",
  "new_hash": "blake3:def456...",
  "expected": "SCHEMA_MISMATCH: interface_hash changed, migration required"
}
```

### State Model
- [ ] State read/write round-trip
- [ ] State versioning works
- [ ] State conflict detection
- [ ] State snapshot creation
- [ ] State restore from snapshot

#### State Golden Vectors
```rust
// tests/vectors/sdk/state_snapshot_v1.cbor
StateSnapshot {
    connector_id: "test-connector",
    version: 1,
    data: {"cursor": "abc123", "last_sync": 1705000000},
    created_at: "2025-01-15T00:00:00Z",
    checksum: "blake3:..."
}

#[test]
fn test_state_conflict_detection() {
    // Concurrent writes must detect conflict
    let state_v1 = connector.get_state().await?;
    let state_v1_clone = state_v1.clone();
    
    // Writer 1 updates
    connector.set_state(state_v1.with_data(json\!({"a": 1})), version: 1).await?;
    
    // Writer 2 tries to update from same version - must fail
    let err = connector.set_state(state_v1_clone.with_data(json\!({"b": 2})), version: 1)
        .await
        .unwrap_err();
    assert\!(matches\!(err, SdkError::StateConflict { .. }));
}
```

### Error Handling
- [ ] SDK errors use error taxonomy
- [ ] Errors include ai_recovery_hint
- [ ] Panic recovery in connector code
- [ ] Timeout handling

#### Error Golden Vectors
```rust
// tests/vectors/sdk/error_taxonomy.json
[
  {
    "code": "FCP-4001",
    "category": "capability",
    "message": "Missing required capability: email.send",
    "ai_recovery_hint": "Request email.send capability from zone administrator",
    "retryable": false
  },
  {
    "code": "FCP-5002",
    "category": "transient",
    "message": "Rate limit exceeded",
    "ai_recovery_hint": "Wait {retry_after_ms}ms before retrying",
    "retryable": true,
    "retry_after_ms": 1000
  }
]

#[test]
fn test_panic_recovery() {
    // Connector panics must be caught and converted to errors
    let mock = MockConnector::new()
        .on_invoke(|| panic\!("connector bug\!"));
    let err = mock.invoke(req).await.unwrap_err();
    assert\!(matches\!(err, SdkError::InternalError { .. }));
    assert\!(err.ai_recovery_hint().is_some());
}
```

### Trait Implementations
- [ ] FcpConnector trait defaults work
- [ ] Capability checking helpers
- [ ] Audit event helpers
- [ ] Receipt creation helpers

## Test File Organization
```
crates/fcp-sdk/tests/
├── sdk_standard_methods.rs     # invoke, simulate, subscribe, etc.
├── sdk_simulate_tests.rs       # Detailed simulate/CostEstimate tests
├── sdk_schema_tests.rs         # Schema generation and validation
├── sdk_state_tests.rs          # State model tests
├── sdk_error_tests.rs          # Error handling tests
├── sdk_trait_tests.rs          # FcpConnector trait tests
└── vectors/
    └── sdk/
        ├── operation_schema_v1.json
        ├── event_schema_v1.json
        ├── state_snapshot_v1.cbor
        ├── simulate_preflight_allow.cbor
        ├── simulate_preflight_deny_capability.cbor
        ├── simulate_preflight_deny_policy.cbor
        ├── cost_estimate_deterministic.cbor
        └── error_taxonomy.json
```

```json
  "timestamp": "RFC3339",
  "test_name": "test_sdk_simulate_preflight_allow",

  },

```

## Integration with E2E Harness
These unit tests form the foundation for E2E scenarios. The mock connectors and golden vectors defined here are reused by:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.4` (E2E Harness)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.36.1` (Happy Path E2E)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.36.2` (Denial Path E2E)

## Acceptance Criteria
- All SDK methods tested with positive and negative cases
- State model is reliable with conflict detection
- Error handling is consistent and includes ai_recovery_hints
- Simulate/CostEstimate tests verify no side effects

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-r6ty","title":"fcp.terraform — Infrastructure as Code Connector","description":"# fcp.terraform - Infrastructure as Code Connector (FCP2)

## Goal
Deliver the FCP2 Terraform connector epic plan for IaC workflows with strict sandboxing, plan/diff safety, and policy-gated apply (dangerous).

## Zone model (mechanical)

<!-- fcp2-single-zone-note -->
**FCP2 single-zone binding note:** A connector instance binds to exactly one `ZoneId` for its entire lifetime.
The connector MUST NOT multiplex multiple zones internally.

Recommended deployment pattern: run separate connector instances in separate zones with different capability grants/policies.

## Strategic Context

### Value Assessment
**Strategic Value Score: 80/100** — Important Tier 3 connector (Infrastructure family).

**Why Critical**: Terraform is the **dominant Infrastructure-as-Code tool**, managing cloud resources across AWS, GCP, Azure, and 1000+ providers. Enables agents to provision infrastructure, review plans, and automate cloud operations. Essential for autonomous DevOps.

### Priority Matrix Position
- **Category**: Specialized Investment (Infrastructure + Safety Critical)
- **Build Phase**: Phase 3 Enterprise (weeks 9-12)

### Pulumi Comparison
- **Terraform**: HCL-based, mature ecosystem, state management
- **Pulumi**: General-purpose languages, same cloud targets
- Both needed for full IaC coverage

---

## Agent Capabilities (Concrete Use Cases)

### Plan & Apply
- Generate infrastructure plans
- Review change diffs
- Apply approved changes
- Destroy resources (controlled)

### State Management
- Read current state
- Import existing resources
- State drift detection
- Resource inventory

### Workspace Management
- List workspaces
- Switch workspaces
- Lock/unlock state

### Examples of Agent Workflows
```
"What will this change affect?"
"Show me the current infrastructure state"
"Apply the approved changes"
"What resources exist in production?"
"Has any infrastructure drifted?"
"Import this existing S3 bucket"
"Create a new staging workspace"
```

---

## Flywheel Ecosystem Synergies

### Kubernetes Connector Synergy
**Full stack automation:**
- Terraform provisions clusters
- K8s connector manages workloads
- Agent handles both layers

### GitHub Integration
**GitOps workflows:**
- PR triggers terraform plan
- Agent reviews plan safety
- Merge triggers apply

### Datadog/Grafana Integration
- Infrastructure metrics correlation
- Provisioning impacts on observability
- Cost monitoring integration

### Agent Mail Integration
- Plan review requests → agent threads
- Apply approval workflows
- Drift notifications

### Stripe Integration
- Cloud cost correlation
- Resource provisioning → billing impact
- Budget enforcement

---

## Technical Implementation Notes

### Execution Modes

#### Terraform CLI Wrapper
- Execute `terraform` commands via subprocess
- Parse plan/apply output
- Handle state file operations

#### Terraform Cloud/Enterprise API
- REST API for cloud workspaces
- Remote state management
- Run management

### Recommended Crates
- `tokio::process` — Command execution
- `serde_json` — JSON output parsing
- `reqwest` — Terraform Cloud API

### Core Operations
```
init → plan → apply
         ↓
      state operations
```

---

## Zone Architecture

### Recommended Zones
| Zone | Description | Capabilities |
|------|-------------|--------------|
| `z:infra:terraform:full` | Full access | All operations including apply |
| `z:infra:terraform:plan` | Plan only | Plan and state read |
| `z:infra:terraform:readonly` | Read-only | State inspection only |
| `z:infra:terraform:workspace:<name>` | Workspace-scoped | Single workspace |

### Safety Boundaries
- Apply is **safety-critical** — requires explicit approval
- Destroy is **highest risk** — double confirmation
- Plan is safe — read-only simulation

---

## Manifest Archetypes (Closed Set)
- `operational` — CLI execution / API calls

---

## External Surface

### NetworkConstraints
Varies by mode:
- **CLI**: Cloud provider APIs (AWS, GCP, Azure endpoints)
- **Terraform Cloud**: `app.terraform.io:443`

### Credential Injection
- Cloud provider credentials via environment
- TFE token via `CredentialId`
- Never write credentials to disk

---

## Capability Model

### Read Operations
- `terraform.state.read` — Read current state
- `terraform.state.list` — List resources
- `terraform.workspaces.read` — List workspaces
- `terraform.plan.read` — View existing plans

### Write Operations
- `terraform.plan.create` — Run terraform plan
- `terraform.apply.execute` — Apply changes (Dangerous)
- `terraform.state.import` — Import resources

### Administrative (Dangerous)
- `terraform.destroy` — Destroy infrastructure
- `terraform.state.remove` — Remove from state
- `terraform.workspaces.delete` — Delete workspace

---

## Operations (Representative)

### Terraform Plan
```bash
terraform plan -out=tfplan -json
```

Parsed output:
```json
{
  "resource_changes": [{
    "address": "aws_instance.web",
    "change": {
      "actions": ["create"],
      "before": null,
      "after": {
        "instance_type": "t3.medium",
        "ami": "ami-12345678"
      }
    }
  }]
}
```

### Terraform Apply
```bash
terraform apply tfplan -json
```

### Read State
```bash
terraform show -json
```

### Terraform Cloud Run (API)
```json
POST /api/v2/runs
{
  "data": {
    "type": "runs",
    "attributes": {
      "message": "Deploy API changes"
    },
    "relationships": {
      "workspace": {
        "data": {"type": "workspaces", "id": "ws-123abc"}
      }
    }
  }
}
```

---

## Safety Considerations

### Plan Review Protocol
1. Agent generates plan
2. Plan must show all changes
3. Human/policy approval required
4. Only then can apply proceed

### Dangerous Operations
| Operation | Risk | Mitigation |
|-----------|------|------------|
| `apply` | High | Require prior plan approval |
| `destroy` | Very High | Double confirmation, no auto-approve |
| `state rm` | High | Audit log, confirmation |
| Import | Medium | Validation of resource match |

### Drift Detection
- Periodic state refresh
- Compare plan output
- Alert on unexpected changes

---

## Terraform Cloud Integration

### Benefits
- Remote state management
- Team collaboration
- Policy enforcement (Sentinel)
- VCS integration

### API Capabilities
- Workspace management
- Run triggering and monitoring
- Variable management
- State version access

---

## Tests

### Unit Tests (Mock-Only)
- Plan output parsing
- State JSON handling
- Change detection logic
- Workspace management

### Integration Tests
- Local terraform with mock providers
- State file operations
- Plan/apply workflow simulation

### E2E Compliance
- Validates NetworkConstraints
- Apply requires approval
- Audit logging complete

---

## Acceptance Criteria
- Passes connector compliance runner (static + dynamic)
- No real infrastructure changes in tests
- Plan generation works
- State reading functional
- Apply requires explicit approval
- Terraform Cloud API works
- Proper audit trail maintained

## Success Criteria
- Connector epic is fully decomposed into implementable beads (manifest/cap map, provisioning, operations, tests, E2E compliance).
- All child beads are implemented and closed, and the connector passes the mechanical compliance runner + its E2E scenarios with structured JSON logs.
- Mechanical guarantees hold: single-zone binding, default-deny capabilities, and NetworkConstraints enforcement; no secret/PII leakage in logs.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-r6ty.1","title":"fcp.terraform: Manifest + Capability Map (FCP2)","description":"# fcp.terraform: Manifest + Capability Map (FCP2)

## Goal
Define the Terraform connector’s **mechanical manifest contract** under FCP2.

Terraform is safety-critical; the manifest must make dangerous operations mechanically obvious.

## Required contents
- Manifest archetypes (closed set):
  - `operational` (CLI invocation; plan/apply/state)
- Operation list + schema IDs.
- Capability families + per-operation required caps.
- NetworkConstraints:
  - default deny
  - explicitly allow only the minimal outbound hosts required for the configured backends/providers
  - optionally require “offline mode” for plan-only environments
- Sandbox profile:
  - strict filesystem allowlist
  - explicit binary allowlist (terraform + minimal helpers)
- State model declaration:
  - plan artifact references + hashes (if persisted)
  - drift checkpoints (if persisted)

## Acceptance criteria
- Another engineer can implement `--manifest` from this bead alone.
- Static compliance can reject:
  - overly-broad egress
  - unsafe filesystem profiles

- Detect/verify Terraform binary:
  - version constraints
  - hash/publisher verification if required by policy

  - AWS creds for S3 backend
  - GCP creds for GCS backend
  - etc.
- Safety mode configuration:
  - default: plan-only
  - apply requires explicit approval gating
  - destroy is disabled unless explicitly enabled by policy
- Validate sandbox filesystem constraints:
  - only allow configured workspace paths

## Acceptance criteria
- `fcp doctor` verifies readiness without leaking secrets.
- Mockable in CI (no real terraform needed for unit tests).

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-s9v","title":"fcp.docusign: Download Signed Documents","description":"# fcp.docusign: Download Signed Documents (FCP2)

## Goal
Allow retrieval of completed, signed documents safely:
- list documents in an envelope
- download document bytes (streamed, bounded)

Signed documents may contain highly sensitive data.

## Scope
- List envelope documents (metadata).
- Download a document.
- Optional: download combined document bundle.

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `docusign.documents.read` (dangerous; sensitive content)

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned.
- Strict IO bounds:
  - max bytes
  - streaming download
  - hash computation for evidence
- Never log document contents.

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `docusign.document.list`
  - `docusign.document.download`

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - download bounds
  - host pinning
  - hash correctness

## Acceptance criteria
- Document downloads are safe-by-default and do not allow unbounded memory usage.

Coordination note: direct Agent Mail contact/message attempts to YellowSnow / ChartreuseCardinal still hit the same transient Agent Mail database error, so I am recording the result here as well.","created_at":"2026-03-25T05:57:06Z"},{"id":1990,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-sb8wb","author":"jemanuel","text":"Deep audit pass landed two concrete cross-cutting fixes.

1. `crates/fcp-streaming/src/health.rs`
- Root cause: `StreamHealthTracker::record_disconnect()` treated `max_reconnect_attempts` as an exclusive upper bound (`>`), which contradicted both the docs and the shared `ReconnectHandler` contract (`attempts < max`).
- Fix: switch the boundary to `>=` so the tracker becomes `Unhealthy` exactly when the reconnect budget is exhausted, not one disconnect later.
- Regression coverage: updated `max_reconnect_attempts_makes_unhealthy`, updated `simulated_max_reconnects_exhaustion`, and added `zero_max_reconnect_attempts_fails_first_disconnect`.
- Verification: `rch exec -- cargo test -p fcp-streaming --lib max_reconnect_attempts_makes_unhealthy` passed; `rch exec -- cargo test -p fcp-streaming --lib zero_max_reconnect_attempts_fails_first_disconnect` passed.

2. `crates/fwc/src/history.rs`
- Root cause: the advertised history env overrides were broken in two ways: malformed env var names (`FWC_max_log_size()_BYTES`, `FWC_max_rotated_files()`) that could not realistically be set, and `HistoryFilter::new()` ignored the documented `FWC_HISTORY_DEFAULT_LIMIT` override entirely.
- Fix: centralize the env var names as `FWC_MAX_LOG_SIZE_BYTES`, `FWC_MAX_ROTATED_FILES`, and `FWC_HISTORY_DEFAULT_LIMIT`; route `HistoryFilter::new()` through a real env-backed `default_limit()` helper; add pure helper-based regression tests for override and fallback behavior.
- Verification: `rch exec -- cargo test -p fwc --lib default_limit_reads_env_override` compiled the crate successfully but ran 0 tests because `history.rs` is only wired into the binary target. Re-running against the binary target exposed unrelated pre-existing `fwc/src/main.rs` compile failures (`try_host_tool_operation_info` / `try_host_mcp_tool_definitions` not in scope), so the new history tests could not be executed end-to-end in this session.

Additional finding not yet patched:
- `crates/fcp-telemetry/src/lib.rs`: `init_telemetry()` promises subsequent calls are no-ops, but unlike `init_telemetry_sync()` it still re-runs logging/metrics/tracing side effects, so repeated or concurrent init can fail spuriously. I attempted to patch this with a serialized early-return guard, but writes to that file failed under filesystem pressure (`ENOSPC`).

Environment notes:
- The workspace volume is effectively full (`df -h .` showed only ~5.8 GiB available on a 100% utilized APFS data volume), which caused transient write failures and blocked formatter rewrites until I reduced the final formatting delta to a tiny manual patch.
- `rustfmt --edition 2024 --check crates/fcp-streaming/src/health.rs crates/fwc/src/history.rs` passed after that manual adjustment.

","created_at":"2026-03-25T06:19:50Z"},{"id":1992,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-sb8wb","author":"TurquoiseFortress","text":"Fresh-eyes review pass found and repaired four concrete issues across fellow-agent changes.

1. connectors/anthropic: base_url validation only checked scheme/host while the client appends fixed /v1 paths. Non-root values like https://api.anthropic.com/v1 or proxy-like URLs could produce malformed request URLs or inconsistent doctor results. Fixed by reusing a single parsed policy in configure + doctor, rejecting path/query/fragment, and adding regression coverage.

2. connectors/docusign: base_url policy accepted arbitrary DocuSign domains like account.docusign.com/oauth/auth even though the client expects a REST API account-root base and appends /{account_id}/... paths. Fixed by requiring non-local endpoints to be /restapi/<version>/accounts roots, rejecting query/fragment, and tightening tests.

3. connectors/gcp: endpoint overrides validated only host/scheme even though the client treats them as plain origins before appending service paths. Fixed by rejecting path/query/fragment on non-local Google API overrides and adding regression coverage for pathful overrides.

4. connectors/docusign: integration tests exposed a contract mismatch where docusign.stream_connect_events was advertised in introspection/tests but still returned an unimplemented error. Fixed by returning a deterministic empty poll result with streaming=true and optional since_ts echo until real Connect polling lands.

Validation via rch:
- cargo fmt -p fcp-anthropic -p fcp-docusign -p fcp-gcp -- --check passed.
- cargo check -p fcp-anthropic -p fcp-docusign -p fcp-gcp --all-targets passed.
- cargo test -p fcp-anthropic --test integration -- --nocapture passed (70 integration tests).
- cargo test -p fcp-docusign --test integration -- --nocapture passed (51 integration tests).
- cargo test -p fcp-gcp -- --nocapture passed (88 unit tests, 9 integration tests).
- cargo clippy -p fcp-anthropic -p fcp-docusign -p fcp-gcp --all-targets --no-deps -- -D warnings is still blocked by an unrelated upstream asupersync compile failure on the worker (missing dispatch_phase0_remote_simulation), not by these connector changes.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-sfa5w","title":"Set correct non-default timeouts for connectors with known latency profiles","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:12:32.916126Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-25T05:25:59.489946Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T05:25:59.489775Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["config","connectors","hardening"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-sfa5w","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-6gtq7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:12:46.903866Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1958,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-sfa5w","author":"jemanuel","text":"DEPENDS ON: manifest [timeouts] schema being available (6gtq7).

Connectors that need non-default timeouts (i.e., 30s is wrong):

LONG-RUNNING (need longer):
- anthropic: 120-300s for inference (client.rs:106 already uses 120s for HTTP, 300s for execution context — but these should come from manifest not code)
- openai: similar to anthropic, inference can take 60-120s
- google-ai: similar, generative AI responses can be slow
- whisper: audio transcription can take minutes for long files (MAX_FILE_SIZE_BYTES=25MB)
- terraform: plan/apply operations can take minutes
- kubernetes: apply/delete operations can take 60s+

SHORT-RUNNING (could use tighter):
- redis: key-value operations should complete in <5s
- elasticsearch: queries should complete in <15s
- duckdb: local queries should be fast

VARIABLE:
- browser: page navigation/screenshot can vary wildly (connector.rs already has 300s wall_clock_timeout_ms)
- s3: upload operations depend on file size

Steps:
1. For each connector above, add a [timeouts] section to manifest.toml with the correct values
2. Remove the hard-coded Duration::from_secs() from client.rs where it duplicates the manifest
3. Verify tests still pass with manifest-driven timeouts","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:12:46Z"}]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-shj4","title":"TEST: fcp-oauth Unit Test Suite","description":"# TEST: fcp-oauth Unit Test Suite

## Scope
Comprehensive unit tests for the OAuth2/OAuth1 authentication library.

## Test Categories

### OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow
- [ ] Authorization URL generation with state/PKCE
- [ ] Token exchange with valid/invalid codes
- [ ] Refresh token flow
- [ ] Token expiration handling
- [ ] Scope validation

### OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials Flow
- [ ] Token acquisition
- [ ] Caching and refresh

### OAuth 1.0a Flow (Twitter/legacy)
- [ ] Request token acquisition
- [ ] User authorization redirect
- [ ] Access token exchange
- [ ] Signature generation (HMAC-SHA1)

### Token Management
- [ ] Token storage/retrieval
- [ ] Token validation
- [ ] Secure token handling (no disk writes)

### Error Handling
- [ ] Invalid client credentials
- [ ] Expired tokens
- [ ] Revoked tokens
- [ ] Network failures
- [ ] Rate limits

## Mocking Strategy
- Use wiremock to mock OAuth provider endpoints
- Test against mock Google, GitHub, Twitter, Slack endpoints
